coredata.dc:title: Advances in multi-dimensional cellulose-based fluorescent carbon dot composites

Zhao, Siyu
Liu, Wei
Han, Xing
Wu, Min
Zhao, Peitao
Ragauskas, Arthur J.

coredata.dc:description: Recently, fluorescent materials composed of carbon dots (CDs) have attracted increasing attention in diverse fields, including the environment, food, biology, and medicine. Cellulose has emerged as a promising class of materials for carriers due to its low cost, fascinating biodegradability, and various morphologies, especially in available different dimensions. By taking advantage of the respective superiority of fluorescent materials and cellulose carriers, the performance of fluorescence composite materials can be optimized and their application may be extended. In this review, the study emphasizes the synthesis and applications of cellulose-based fluorescent materials with different dimensions. Moreover, some effective strategies and potential challenges for further development of cellulose-based fluorescent materials are discussed. A deeper understanding will provide a guide for the materials with ideal fluorescent performances.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: *The diameter of 0D nanoparticles ranges from 1 nm to 100 nm, 1D fibers are linear materials less than 1 μm in diameter but more than a few microns in length, 2D films vary from a few nanometers to micrometers in thickness, but its length and width are usually more than a few microns, 3D hydrogels and aerogels are cylinders that is more than a few microns in length, width and height.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Recently, fluorescent materials composed of carbon dots (CDs) have attracted increasing attention in diverse fields, including the environment, food, biology, and medicine. Cellulose has emerged as a promising class of materials for carriers due to its low cost, fascinating biodegradability, and various morphologies, especially in available different dimensions. By taking advantage of the respective superiority of fluorescent materials and cellulose carriers, the performance of fluorescence composite materials can be optimized and their application may be extended. In this review, the study emphasizes the synthesis and applications of cellulose-based fluorescent materials with different dimensions. Moreover, some effective strategies and potential challenges for further development of cellulose-based fluorescent materials are discussed. A deeper understanding will provide a guide for the materials with ideal fluorescent performances.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence is a photoluminescence phenomenon that is commonly found in the nature and our daily life. There are many types of fluorescent materials, such as organic fluorescent dyes, lanthanide chelates, clusters of metal nanoparticles, and fluorescent small molecule clusters [1–5]. After the fluorescent substance is excited by a specific wavelength of light, the electrons in the excited state will release energy back returning to the ground state to maintain stability. When the energy is released in the form of photons, fluorescence is produced. Many excellent advantages of this process have been acknowledged such as high luminescence efficiency, stable physicochemical properties and easy chemical modifications are observed in fluorescence substance, consequently bringing tremendous applications, e.g. chemical sensing, fuel cells, biology, and imaging [6–9]. However, some issues including facile oxidative bleaching, overlapping excitation and emission spectra, and low sensitivity which severely limit wide applications of these fluorescent materials (see Scheme 1 ).

As a new type of fluorescent material, carbon dots (CDs) have been widely discerned by researchers since it was first reported in 2004 [10]. Compared with traditional fluorescent materials, CDs not only overcome the shortcomings of photobleaching and instability of traditional fluorescent materials, but also have the advantages of low toxicity, high stability, good biocompatibility, tunable fluorescence, abundant functional groups and easy modification, and thus they are widely used in chemical sensing, catalysis, drug delivery, and bioimaging [11–13]. More importantly, the surface of CDs has abundant and controllable functional groups, such as hydroxyl, carboxyl, amino and amide groups. These functional groups have important effects on the optical and chemical properties of CDs and can be tailored by controlling reaction conditions of CDs synthesis such as solvent, temperature, and pH [14–18]. According to the carbon core structure, surface groups, and properties of CDs, CDs have been classified into four types: graphene quantum dots (GQDs), carbon quantum dots (CQDs), carbon nanodots (CNDs), and carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) [19]. However, the fluorescence properties of CDs are limited due to aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) effects [20]. Therefore, the immobilization of CDs may effectively increase the fluorescence of CDs and confer a variety of applications to composites, depending on the nature of the immobilized carrier.

Cellulose is one of the most abundant biodegradable and renewable organic substances in nature. It is a macromolecular polymer composed of glucopyranose units linked by β-(1,4)-glycosidic bonds. Cellulose with different molecular weights and crystallinities from plants, algae, or bacterial tissues which can be normally utilized as zero-dimensional nanoparticles, one-dimensional fibers [21], two-dimensional films [22,23], three-dimensional hydrogel [24], and aerogel [25]. Furthermore, these cellulose resources can be also easily modified due to the C-2, 3, and 6 hyroxyl groups along with the reducing end hydroxyl group and terminal C-4 OH [26]. These cellulose resources with different dimensions are promising immobilization carriers to produce various dimensional composites. These different dimensional cellulose-based composites have unique advantages such as small size, large surface, good mechanical, excellent optical, and thermal properties. Hence these materials are being widely been used in chemistry, environmental, food, materials, medical and electronic fields [27–29]. For example, zero-dimensional particles and one-dimensional fibers are easy to enter the biological cells because of their small size, and are the ideal material for biomedicine and bio-imaging [30]. Two-dimensional films are beneficial to being patterned for visual observation and normally used in packaging, building, and flexible electronic fields [23,31–34]. The porous network structure of three-dimensional hydrogels and aerogels are rich with binding sites and makes the material easy to modify and compound with fluorescent CDs. These modified hydrogels and aerogels are normally used for adsorption, energy storage, and sensor detection [24,26]. Therefore, in order to solve the aggregation induced quenching effect of CDs, cellulose with different sizes are a promising carrier to immobilize CDs and produce different cellulose-based fluorescent composites.

Cellulose-based fluorescent composites were prepared by linking CDs with cellulose for the first time in 2014 [35], and research interest has only increased since then. These materials have found applications in environmental science, security and anti-counterfeiting, bioimaging, and medical drug delivery applications [36–39]. Herein, in this study, we mainly focus on the combination mode and properties of cellulose-based fluorescent CDs composites with different dimensions, application of cellulose-based fluorescent composites, as well as future developments. This review will provide guidance for the research progress of cellulose-based fluorescent CDs composites.

The preparation of cellulose-based fluorescent materials with different dimensions primarily consists of three steps, including the preparation of fluorescent CDs, the production of cellulose with different fibril sizes, and the composite of CDs and cellulose. In addition, some intermediate processes, such as separation, purification and drying, are commonly involved in compositing the cellulose-based fluorescent composites. These preparation conditions exert significant effects on the performance and applications of the fluorescent composites.

The precursors for preparing CDs are obtained from a wide range of sources, and the preparation methods are divided into “top-down” and “bottom-up” approaches, according to precursors, as shown in Fig. 1 . Each of them has its own advantages.

“Top-down” is a method of fragmenting large carbon-based precursors (e.g., carbon black, graphite, activated carbon, and carbon nanotubes) into smaller molecular structures by laser ablation, arc discharge, and electrochemical oxidation [49,50]. Green, yellow, and orange fluorescent nanoparticles were initially produced from the derivatives of single wall carbon nanotubes in the process of preparing carbon nanotubes with arc discharge in 2004 [10], as shown in Fig. 2 (a). Multicolor fluorescent CDs with a uniform size of 2–6 nm and good water solubility could also been obtained by passivating industrial activated carbon with nitric acid, as shown in Fig. 2 (b) [51]. As the technology developed, nano sized graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were synthesized in 2018, using carbon nano-onions as raw materials through chemical oxidation and one-step pulsed laser ablation in deionized water, respectively, as shown in Fig. 2 (c)–(e) [52]. Subsequently, GQDs are shown to have a blue shifted emission, which are related to their particle size and surface functional groups.

The “Bottom-up” method mainly refers to the carbonization and polymerization of small molecules to generate CDs, primarily including chemical vapor deposition, solvothermal, hydrothermal, and microwave procedures [30,53–58]. Both hydrothermal and solvothermal methods, which are the most common synthesis methods, use a stainless-steel high-pressure reaction kettle lined with polytetrafluoroethylene to prepare CDs [59–61]. Blue, green, and red CDs, correspondingly denoted as b-CDs, g-CDs and r-CDs, were obtained using citric acid and urea as precursors and water, dimethyl acetamide, or dimethyl formamide as solvents, respectively [62]. The fluorescence emission peaks of the b-, g- and r-CDs are located at approximately 476 nm, 543 nm and 634 nm, respectively, as shown in Fig. 3 (a)-(c). The average diameters of the b-, g- and r-CDs are 3.2 nm, 4.1 nm and 6.7 nm, respectively, as shown in Fig. 3 (d)–(f). In addition, the UV–vis absorption spectrum, time-resolved attenuation spectrum, and ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy data for CDs indicate that the degree of graphitization of CDs and the size of the conjugated sp2 domain can be adjusted in different solvents. In addition to small molecules that can be used as precursors, biomass has been used as a high-quality feedstock for the preparation of CDs. Our group's research found that lignocellulose-based carbon quantum dots (GX-CQDs) were produced using a one-pot hydrothermal method with lignin and xylan as a raw material, as shown in Fig. 3 (g) [63]. The GX-CQDs have a layered core-shell multilayer graphene structure surrounded by oxygen-containing functional groups. Compared with the hydrothermal method and solvothermal method, the microwave method has the characteristics of a short reaction time and high efficiency. Liao et al. simply and rapidly synthesized fluorescent CDs in 4 min through microwave-assisted pyrolysis of citric acid and 3,3′-diaminobenzidine (DAB) [64]. The size of the CDs obtained is relatively uniform, approximately 2.6 nm, and the fluorescence quantum yield (QY) is up to 8%. However, the yield of CDs prepared using hydrothermal, solvothermal or microwave methods alone is relatively low [65]. The combination of microwave and hydrothermal methods for the preparation of CDs can result in a yield on a kilogram scale as shown in Fig. 3 (h) [46]. After placing o-phenylenediamine in a household microwave oven and reacting it at 200 W for 20 min the product mixture was obtained and then transferred to an autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 12 h. Near infrared region fluorescent CDs with a mass yield greater than 96% was obtained.

The fluorescence of CDs in cellulose-based fluorescent composites can not only can result in cellulose to have fluorescence but also can determine the optical and electrical properties of composite materials. Hence, the application field of these composites is an active field of study. Both effective conjugate size and surface charge distribution play key roles in the fluorescence emission of CDs. The use of different precursors leads to the preparation of CDs containing different surface functional groups, including hydroxyl, carboxyl, amino, and amide or carbonyl groups, and the obtained CDs have different luminescence characteristics [66]. The fluorescence properties of CDs can be effectively improved by doping. Common doping elements include N, S, and P, in addition, some metal elements, such as K+, Zn2+, Mn2+, Co2+, Ni2+ also have been used [40,67–72]. Hola et al. [40] synthesized panchromatic CD mixtures from citric acid and urea in formamide and then separated individual fluorescent CDs by column chromatography based on the difference of CDs charges, as shown in Fig. 4 . XPS, FT-IR, Raman spectroscopy and DFT calculations confirm that the increase of graphite N content in CDs leads to the photoluminescence separation, the increasing of surface negative charge and the redshift trend. Furthermore, highly efficient red-emitting nitrogen-doped carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) were obtained using hydrothermal synthesis with o-phenylenediamine and nitric acid as precursors [73]. The optimal emission wavelength of CPDs is approximately 630 nm. These CPDs have excellent water dispersion, good biocompatibility, narrow bandwidth emission, excitation-independent emission, and a high QY (10.83% in water, 31.54% in ethanol). Therefore, the preparation of high-fluorescent performance CDs is very important.

Cellulose is widely present in nature in the form of microfibrils in the cell walls of wood and plants, algae, and bacterial tissues, as shown in Fig. 5 (a). Cellulose fibers are separated into fibers of progressively decreasing diameter, from less than 100 μm to 2–4 nm [74], as shown in Fig. 5 (b). From the perspective of the diameter, cellulose can be reduced to cellulose microfibers (CMF, >100 nm) and nanocellulose (NC, <100 nm). CMF can be prepared by mechanical grinding, TEMPO oxidation, low eutectic solvent and other methods, and the resulting CMF has good tensile performance and toughness [75–77]. However, CMF usually has some problems such as low purity and uneven particle size distribution. Nanocellulose is mainly extracted from plant fibers or bacterial pericapsules and consists of three types: nanofibrillated cellulose (NFC), cellulose nanocrystal (CNC), and bacterial nanocellulose (BNC) [33,74,78,79]. NFC is usually prepared by mechanical grinding and has a large aspect ratio with diameters ranging from 1 to 100 nm and lengths ranging from 500 to 2000 nm. The grinding energy consumption and the size uniformity of NFC can be significantly improved by the effects of endoglucanase-assisted mechanical grinding and high-pressure homogenization, based on our group's previous investigation [80]. Compared with mechanical grinding alone, these two treatment processes can improve the tensile strength and transparency of NFC membrane from 132.31 MPa to 27.52% to 177.99 MPa and 61.75%, respectively. CNC is commonly produced by acid hydrolysis of cellulosic fibers, and much literatures reflect the work in this area [81–83], which involves the usage of acid catalysts such as sulfuric, phosphoric, and hydrochloric acids. In this preparation process, changing the concentration of acid can obtain different properties of CNC such as different yield, crystal shape or length-diameter ratio, and crystallinity and degree of sulfation [83–85]. As for BNC, it was usually synthesized by Gram-negative and Gram-positive bacteria [86,87].

Although fibrillated cellulose products have good application prospects, most of them are limited because of their poor solubility, water barrier properties, and high energy consumption. Efforts are underway to further improve the properties of fibrillated cellulose for usage in practical applications. The hydroxyl group is a targeted reactive group on the cellulose chain, and it is easily modified by esterification and etherification reactions to obtain different derivatives with excellent properties, such as cellulose acetate (CA), carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC), and ethyl cellulose (EC) [91,92]. The monomeric structure of CA consists of anhydrous glucose molecules linked by β-1,4-glycosidic bonds. The C-2, 3, and 6 hydroxyl groups of the glucose unit of cellulose are replaced by acetyl groups, as shown in Fig. 5 (c) [88]. By adjusting the ratio of cellulose to acetic anhydride, CA with different degrees of acetyl substitution can be prepared, which results in differences of solubility and hydrophobicity. Moreover, compared with cellulose, CA is not only more biocompatible and biodegradable but also has higher thermal stability and better mechanical properties. CMC is an important cellulose ether that is usually obtained in a two-step reaction, as shown in Fig. 5 (d) [89]. Alkali cellulose is first extracted by reacting wood residue, paper residue, corn husk and other crude fibers with a sodium hydroxide solution. Then, CMC was prepared by reacting alkali cellulose with monochloroacetic acid or ethanol with sodium monochloroacetic acid. CMC has polyelectrolyte property, therefore, it easily binds to different ions in solution. As for EC, it is a moderately ethylated cellulose ether obtained by reacting alkali cellulose with ethyl chloride. It is another important cellulose ether with the similar preparation method of CMC. EC is insoluble in water; however, it is soluble in a variety of organic solvents, including ethanol, methanol, toluene, chloroform, and ethyl acetate, as shown in Fig. 5 (e) [90]. In conclusion, both cellulose and its derivatives exist in the form of polymers, which can be prepared by physical or chemical cross-linking to form hydrogels and aerogels with excellent properties [26,93]. Cellulose and its derivatives commonly function as a skeleton in several composites, and the chemical structure of cellulose affects how it binds to CDs and the mechanical and optical properties of cellulose-based materials, further affecting the application of composites.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and properties of cellulose-based CDs composites with different dimensions
ce:section.ce:para: The abundant functional groups on the surface of CDs can react with the functional groups on the surface of cellulose and its derivatives through graft copolymerization, electrostatic interactions, covalent coupling, and laser printing, all of which are attached to the cellulose backbone. Based on the properties of different sizes of cellulose, the composites may be processed into different macroscopic morphologies, mainly including quasi-zero-dimensional nanoparticles (we named its as 0D nanoparticles), quasi-one-dimensional fibers (we named it as 1D fibers), 2D films, 3D hydrogel and 3D aerogel. Cellulose-based CDs composites with different dimensions have different morphologies and applications. Table 1 provides a summary about the properties and preparation of cellulose-based fluorescent CDs composites.

Nanoparticles (NPs) are the basic substances in the field of nanotechnology [106]. The size of nanoparticles ranges from 1 nm to 100 nm. Hybridized nanoparticle composites prepared with cellulose or cellulose derivatives and CDs have a wide range of applications. C-dots/CNC NPs with chirality, cellular biocompatibility, bright fluorescence and high cell absorption characteristics were prepared by mixing CNC suspension with boric acid solution and ethylenediamine, and refluxing at 110 °C for 12 h, as shown in Fig. 6 (a) [94]. The average size of the C-dots/CNC NPs is 2.8 ± 2.3 nm, as indicated in Fig. 6 (b). Upon irradiation with UV light, the hybridized C-dots/CNC NPs exhibit strong left circularly polarized light emission with an anisotropy factor gLum = 0.2 at the maximum emission wavelength, which is significantly higher than the reported value of hybridized CNC-based fluorescent NPs, as shown in Fig. 6 (c). Moreover, the normalized proliferation index of mcf 7 cells for the hybridized C-dots/CNC NPs is greater than 100%, leading to the NPs easily entering small volumes of material such as cell. Therefore, nanoparticles can be used in the biological field. In addition to these accomplishments, magnetic fluorescence nanoparticles (MFNPs) were successfully synthesized by layer-on-layer self-assembly method with chitosan containing CDs@Fe3O4 NPs or carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC) containing CDs@Fe3O4 nanoparticles, as shown in Fig. 6 (d)–(f) [107]. The MFNPs can be used as a protein immobilization system because of the excellent fluorescence and magnetic response, expanded surface area, and enhanced immobilization and release performance, as shown in Fig. 6 (g). However, due to their small size, CDs still easily agglomerate during the preparation of NPs, resulting in varying degrees of fluorescence quenching. Therefore, studies in this field must solve the problem of fluorescence quenching caused by their size and molecular associations.

1D fiber materials have lateral dimensions less than 100 nm. Electrostatic spinning is commonly used to prepare one-dimensional nanocellulose composite fluorescent materials, and it has become the main method to prepare 1D nanofibers because of the advantage of a low manufacturing cost and the ability of the fibers to maintain stable structure [108]. Shamsipour et al. prepared a nucleus/chitosan-polyethylene oxide-CQDs/carboxymethylcellulose-polyvinyl alcohol (CS-PEO-CQDs/CMC-PVA) 1D nanofiber through coaxial electrostatic spinning [41], which were applied in the field of tumor therapy. During these experiments, CQDs were activated by a carbodiimide crosslinker (EDC/NHS) and covalently linked with TMZ to prepare TMZ-CQDs. The TMZ-CQDs were mixed with CS-PEO as the nuclear structure for electrospinning. The shell structure of the composite was prepared by mixing CMC with PVA. The two blends of nuclear and shell materials were placed in different nozzles to finally obtain coaxial nanofibers, as shown in Fig. 7 (a). Based on the fact that magnetic fibers not only act as a metal ion sensors, but also can remove magnetic metal particles, the nanofiber/Fe/CQDs nanocomposites were prepared by combining CQDs and iron nanoparticles with the nanofibers [109], as shown in Fig. 7 (b)–(c). In this preparation process, Fe iron nanoparticles were initially produced by ball milling, nanofibers were prepared from cellulose acetate by electrospinning, and CQDs were obtained by hydrothermal method with Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave. The nanofiber/Fe/CQDs nanocomposites have both magnetic and photoluminescent properties, which lead to electron transfer and PL reduction with the addition of Hg2+ and Pb2+ ions. Thus, the composites are novel magnetic nanofibers used as a sensor for Hg2+ and Pb2+ ions. These fibers are suitable for the detection of hazardous heavy metals in industrial waste water and the recycling of magnetic materials. In addition to electrostatic spinning, the covalent coupling method is also utilized to produce 1D nanofibers. After oxidizing the hydroxyl group on cellulose C6 to form a carboxyl group using 2,2,6,6-tetramethyl-1-piperidinyloxy (TEMPO), the nitrogen-doped CQDs were adsorbed onto the oxidized cellulose via a silane coupling agent KH-560 to obtain cellulose/CQDs composite (CKHC) [110], as shown in Fig. 7 (d). The cellulose/CQDs 1D composite makes the CQDs easier to recycle than pure CQDs, which is beneficial to reuse the CQDs and avoids waste and environmental pollution. In general, 1D fiber materials have excellent mechanical properties and thermal stability due to the inherited features of the fiber, and optical properties derived from CDs, but the application of one-dimensional fibers is still relatively limited.

Paper and films are the most widely used forms of cellulose. The fibers are interlaced with each other to form a structurally dense film during the deposition of the fiber suspension. Most of the reported CDs exhibit bright fluorescence emission in solution, but their fluorescence intensity is decreased or fluorescence is quenched in solid-state, due to the energy depletion within the conjugated π-domain in solid state, producing aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) phenomenon [59]. Embedding CDs into a solid-state matrix and establishing the chemical bond between CDs and the matrix are an effective method to enhance the photoluminescence of solid-state CDs. An excellent red fluorescent transparent cellulose film was synthesized by covalent bonding of regenerated cellulose acetate (CA) film with red CDs (R-CDs), as shown in Fig. 8 (a) [95]. R-CDs containing carboxyl and amino groups form a strong covalent bond with the CA film, resulting in a transparent film with red fluorescence (R-CDs@cellulose). The formation of the new chemical bond results in a blueshift of the emission peak from 645 nm to 625 nm. Moreover, the formation of chemical bonds reduces the surface defects of R-CDs and produces a more efficient method of radiative transition. For example, the QY of R-CDs@cellulose composites is increased by more than a factor of 4 compared with the solid-state R-CDs. Solid phosphors (CDs@cellulose) with excellent fluorescence properties can be also prepared by hydrogen bonding between CDs and cellulose derivatives [111]. The solid phosphor of CDs@cellulose has good film-forming property and can be prepared into fluorescent film. The QY (71.5%) of CDs@cellulose film is higher than that of solid-state CDs (0.17%), as shown in Fig. 8 (b). Additionally, cellulose based fluorescent CDs composites had been prepared by combining hybrid dots (CDs/silicon) with hydroxyethyl cellulose (HEC) binder [112], as shown in Fig. 8 (c). Interestingly, as an ink binder, ethyl cellulose with a CD can effectively prevent fluorescent quenching of hybrid dots, leading to the composite being successfully applied in anti-counterfeiting fluorescent ink. Through screen printing technology, the fluorescent ink is used to transfer the customized pattern to the cellulose paper. It is worth noting that the fluorescent color of these patterns can be switched by adjusting the pH of the environment, so that the prepared ink has good concealment performance. The new fluorescent hybrid dots/HEC ink is expected to be used in high-level security applications, including food packaging, clothing, and documents.

The composite films prepared by implanting fluorescent CDs on the surface of cellulose have not only superior optical properties but also UV-shielding and electrical properties [42,96]. By hybridizing TEMPO-treated oxidized nanocellulose (ONC) and zinc oxide (ZnO) with functionalized CDs, CDs–ONC–ZnO hybrid fluorescent films with UV blocking properties were obtained [97]. The hybridized CDs–ONC–ZnO fluorescent films have a better UV-shielding effect than the undoped NC-ZnO composite films, and the incorporation of CDs substantially reduces the cost of mass-produced UV-blocking films. Similar findings were obtained with cellulose nanocrystalline/CDs composite films which can be used to filter ultraviolet light in glass windows, due to the conjugate structure of CDs achieving effective absorption of UV light, as shown in Fig. 8 (d) [32]. Additionally, the presence of more charged functional groups on the surface of CDs substantially improves the electrical conductivity of CNF@CDs films [98]. Flexible CNF@GQD hybrid films with good fluorescence effects and conductivity were prepared by Xiong et al. [31]. Since the cross-linked network structure of CNF provides a stable loading point for GQDs and the GQDs have large specific capacitance, high energy density and excellent cycling stability of CNF@GQDs hybrid films are obtained. Simultaneously, the CNF@GQDs hybrid films have a long discharge time at high current densities and exhibit a capacitance retention of more than 93% after 5000 cycles at various current densities. Because of the nontoxic nature of the carrier membrane and GQDs nanoparticles, the composite membrane has a wide range of applications not only in the field of optical and electronic devices but also in biomedicine [37,86,113].

In conclusion, in 2D films, CDs may be evenly distributed on the surface of composites by combining CDs with paper or membranes, which weakens the agglomerate effect as a result of the CDs nanometer size and increases the fluorescence intensity. On the other hand, CDs combined with the film endow the membrane with much more promising properties, such as fluorescence, UV-shielding performance, and conductive performance. These advantages allow 2D composite films to have a wide range of applications. However, due to the poor water resistance of thin films, some applications of composite materials are limited. Therefore, research on hydrophobic fluorescent cellulose membranes will be a very active topic in the next few years.

The surface of cellulose is rich in hydroxyl groups, which easily forms a hydrogen bonding network [24,114–116]. Through hydrothermal processes, physical and chemical cross-linking, cellulose is dispersed and gelatinized, and cellulose hydrogels are obtained [99,116–118]. The hydrogels have a three-dimensional cross-linked network structure that swell in aqueous solution by absorbing a large amount of water and maintain a specific shape without dissolving. The three-dimensional network structure of hydrogels by physical cross-linking provides rich binding sites and transport channels for CDs, which may increase the loading of CDs and the binding force. The fluorescent nanofiber hydrogels synthesized by hydrothermal method from a mixture of polyethylene imine (PEI) and CNC at low temperature have good mechanical properties with Young's modulus greater than 200 kPa and good fluorescence, as shown in Fig. 9 (a) [99]. The fluorescence property of nanofiber hydrogels is caused by the formation of CDs during the hydrothermal process. The hydrogel structure is formed by two interactions between CNC and CDs, containing the hydrophobic force between the hydrophobic faces of CNC and the core and alkyl groups of CDs and the hydrogen bonding between the amino groups of CDs and the hydroxyl groups on the CNC surfaces. Simultaneously, the structure of the hydrogel is tailored by adjusting the ratio between PEI and CNC.

Hydrogels prepared by chemical crosslinking with the porous structure and abundant chemical groups on the surface of carboxyl, amino and amide also make the cellulose-based fluorescent composites have high adsorption capacity. For example, a new chitosan (CS)-based fluorescent hydrogel modified with TEMPO-oxidized cellulose nanofibers (TOCN), titanate nanofibers (TN) and CDs can not only effectively detect Cr (VI) in water, but also achieve the effect of removing Cr (VI) through adsorption [100]. In this study, TOCN with carboxyl groups and CDs with amino groups were used to prepare TOCN-CDs by a condensation reaction. Then, the TOCN-CDs, TN, and CS were mixed in an acetic acid solution to form fluorescent hydrogels through a glutaraldehyde cross-linking reaction at room temperature, as shown in Fig. 9 (b). The obtained fluorescent hydrogel has good adsorption performance and achieves rapid visual linear detection of Cr (VI) at concentrations ranging from 10 to 80 mg/L.

Fluorescence hydrogels prepared by covalent bonding method not only have good fluorescence properties, but also have good adsorption effect and sensitivity [101]. For example, fluorescent N-doped CDs/CNF hydrogel (NCDs/CNF-gel) with excellent mechanical properties could be prepared by condensation polymerization reaction, as shown in Fig. 9 (c) [102]. The resulting fluorescent cellulose hydrogel has superior injectability and pressure resistance and can resist acids and bases. In the preparation process, NCDs also can be used as a small molecule cross-linking agent between CNF to improve the mechanical strength of composite hydrogel because NCDs increases the number of cross-linked hydrogen bonds. A lignin-based hydrogel with high strength was synthesized by free radical polymerization reaction to introduce carboxylated cellulose nanofibril (CCNs) and CDs, as shown in Fig. 9 (d) [103]. The prepared lignin-based fluorescent hydrogel has a three-dimensional porous structure with high sensitivity to Cr6+. The porous structure of the hydrogel provides amply active sites and ion transport channels for metal ions, which improves the adsorption performance of hydrogel, increases the stability of the fluorescence signal, and enhances the detection capability. In conclusion, the properties of cellulose-based hydrogel give the composite material a great adsorption capacity and improve the fluorescence intensity of composite material, which will open up much new application fields for the fluorescence composite material.

Aerogels are obtained by drying and removing the liquid from cellulose-based hydrogels. This process mainly includes gelation, aging, and drying [25,93,119–121]. The drying process exerts an important effect on the morphology and structure of the aerogel. Low-temperature supercritical drying and freeze drying are the most commonly used polymer gel drying techniques. The aerogel obtained using these two techniques have good pore structures. A porous aerogel was obtained by qualitative freeze-drying using CNC chemical crosslinking with poly (methyl vinyl ether-co-maleic acid) (PMVEMA) and poly (ethylene glycol) (PEG) [121]. The BET results indicate that the cross-linked aerogel has a large surface area and promising pore volume. The good pore structure of an aerogel can increase the amount of grafting of chemical groups or matter, which is very beneficial as a natural skeleton to composite CDs due to covalently bonding [104]. A CNF/CD-based fluorescent aerogel was obtained by the covalently bonding between carboxylated CNF and aminated CDs, resulting in the structural stability of the aerogel [43], as shown in Fig. 10 (a). The prepared aerogel is not only characterized by a very low denisty, porosity, and high specific surface area but also has good mechanical properties and structural stability (self-healing properties) in water. The maximum grafting number of CDs on the CNF-based aerogel was 113 mg/g, and bright blue fluorescence was emitted under UV irradiation. The advantages of aerogel is not only manifested in the 3D pore structure, recently, compressible aerogel with anisotropy has been reported [122]. Reduced-graphene oxide (rGO)/CDs/CMF terpolymer was used to synthesize compressible layered porous carbon aerogel by bidirectional freezing casting method, as shown in Fig. 10 (b). A fluorescent aerogel with both adsorption and detection of Cr (VI) was also obtained by immersing amino-based CDs (CDs-NH2) into polyethylenimine (PEI)/carboxymethylated cellulose (CMC), named as CPC aerogel [123]. The adsorption experiments confirm that the CPC aerogel has good Cr (VI) extraction ability, and the maximum adsorption capacity for Cr (VI) was 354.61 mg/g. After the removal of Cr (VI) using the CPC aerogel, the water quality meets the level of industrial water safety standards. More importantly, the chromium-containing CPC aerogel was further vulcanized and could use for solar steam power generation with an evaporation rate of ≈1.31 kg/(m−2 h−1) after a single exposure to solar radiation. The results of this study provide new directions for the detection of heavy metal ions and the application of adsorbed materials.

As for CDs in 3D aerogel fluorescence composites, high photoluminescence CDs can yield composites with good fluorescence intensity. For example, Yuan et al. used highly photoluminescent CDs and renewable natural carboxymethylcellulose nanofibers (CM-CNF) to design and synthesize a fluorescent aerogel (FA) with a 3D net-like structure, as shown in Fig. 10 (c) [105]. In the preparation process, the CDs were first prepared using a hydrothermal method, then CM–CNF–CDs were generated by N-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-N′-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC)/N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) coupling chemistry combined CDs with CM-CNF. Finally, a FA with excellent adsorption property was obtained through free radical polymerization between N′, N-methylenebisacrylamide (MBA) and CM–CNF–CDs. The adsorption process was described by the adsorption model using the pseudo-second-order kinetic and Freundlich model. The maximum adsorption capacity of Cr6+ by FA reaches 433.5 mg/g. In conclusion, similar to cellulose-based hydrogels, cellulose-based aerogel has a good 3D network structure and three-dimensional pores, which can be used widely for the detection and adsorption of metal ions. In addition, because of their light weight, aerogel may be used in aerospace, construction, gas adsorption, drug-controlled slow release, and hydrogen energy storage applications.

As summarized, CNF has a great potential as a carrier for CDs, allowing the integration of small molecules into predesigned cellulose materials in the synthesis of cellulose-based fluorescent materials. Some application fields of above cellulose-based fluorescent materials with different dimensions have been exploited, such as in detection, rapid testing, anti-counterfeiting, and biological imaging, as shown in Fig. 11 .

Ions such as Na+, K+, Ca2+, Cl−, Mg2+, and Fe2+ are important electrolytes in the body and play important roles. However, with the rapid development of industrial society, some nonessential ions, including F−, Fe3+, Pb2+, Hg+, Cd2+, and Cr6+, appear in various types of foods and living organisms, which cannot be metabolized by the human body and will be enriched in the body through the food chain, inducing the development of corresponding diseases in humans [125,126]. Traditional heavy metal ion detection methods, such as atomic absorption or atomic emission spectrometry, inductively coupled plasma, mass spectrometry, anodic stripping voltammetry and capillary electrophoresis, usually require expensive instruments, complex sample preparation processes and operation processes, leading to on-site and real-time detection being difficult to achieve [127]. Indeed, ion detection is one of the most widely used applications of cellulose-based fluorescent CD composites. The fluorescence of CDs in composite may be quenched or enhanced by specific ions, thus enabling the visual detection of ions. Cellulose-based fluorescent materials have been prepared as fluorescent sensors, which have the advantages of convenience and speed, and are widely used to detect pollutants, especially heavy metal ions [44,128]. A sensitive sensing 2D film was composed with pyrene-boronic acid (PyB) and CDs, named as CPC film [129]. Due to the unique selectivity for boric acid-fluoride, the CPC film can detect fluoride ions (F−) by a response of blue fluorescent, as shown in Fig. 12(a and b)fig12 . Researchers have looked more deeply and determined that, the fluorescence intensity of the fluorescence composite material and the concentration of the detected ion can be determined by a linear relationship. For example, when CDs were immobilized on cellulose acetate film, a film (CDs/CA film) with sensitivity to Fe3+ in solution was obtained [130]. Then, the Stern-Volmer equation was used to describe the linear relationship between the fluorescence response of CDs and Fe3+ concentration. The linear correction range is 0.1–1.0 mmol, and the detection limit is 0.03 mmol. Additionally, the CDs/CA film can be reused by simple drying, and its fluorescence properties remain largely unchanged after 10 cycles.

Hydrogels are more widely used to detect ions in a water environment than 2D films. CQDs extracted from glucosamine were combined with functionalized alginate (ALg) and CNF hydrogel to prepare fluorescent ALg/CQDs hydrogel and CNF/CQDs hydrogel, respectivel [131]. Comparing the two fluorescent hydrogels, both ALg/CQDs and CNF/CQDs hydrogels display high sensitivity to Fe3+ ions and gold nanoparticles in aqueous solution, as shown in Fig. 12 (c)–(e). Guo et al. prepared an excellent fluorescent hydrogel (FNH) to detect and absorb different heavy metal ions [132]. In the experiment, carboxymethylated cellulose nanofibrils (CM-CNF) and CDs were combined to prepare CM–CNF–CDs, and then potassium persulfate (PPS) was used as an initiator to cross-link CM–CNF–CDs with acrylic acid (AA), acrylamide (AM) and N, N-methylenebisacrylamide (MBA) to prepare a fluorescent hydrogel (FNH). When the fluorescent hydrogel was used as an optical sensor for heavy metal ion detection, the natural framework of CNF improves the diffusion and aggregation speed of heavy metal ions due to the 3D spatial network of nanocellulose hydrogel. The high density of amino groups, carboxyl groups, and hydroxyl groups in the fluorescent hydrogel promote the accumulation and adsorption of heavy metal ions, as shown in Fig. 12 (f). At the same time, the maximum adsorption capacity of Fe3+, Ba2+, Pb2+, and Cu2+ reach 769 mg/g, 212 mg/g, 2056 mg/g, and 1246 mg/g, respectively, as shown in Fig. 12 (g). However, the development of this field has encountered some challenges, especially the detection of trace elements, which is difficult due to the inconspicuous visual detection and the discrimination of fluorescence quenching. Moreover, although hydrogel composites can adsorb and remove some ions, they are not widely used due to their poor accessibility and reusability. These problems will be the focus of future research. If a gel or film with selective transmittance is prepared, ion detection will be efficient and accurate.

Human life is closely related to organic matter. Some organic compounds are essential to our life, but others are harmful. Therefore, the corresponding organic compounds must be monitored using several technologies. Glucose is the main energy source and metabolic intermediate of living cells, and its content in the blood plays an important role in the prevention of diseases, particularly diabetes and its various complications. A novel fiber-optic glucose biosensor based on the sensitive film of CQDs-glucose oxidase/cellulose acetate (CA) composite was prepared [133]. The optical fiber biosensor has high sensitivity and reproducibility for low concentrations of glucose. At the micromolar and nanomolar levels, the rate of change in the fluorescence intensity of the biosensor is linearly related to the glucose concentration, and the relationship between the relative fluorescence intensity ratio and glucose concentration is in the range of 10–200 μmol/L and 10–100 nmol/L, respectively, which was consistent with the modified Stern-Volmer equation.

The freshness of food has an important effect on human health. Adding preservatives can effectively reduce the spoilage of food. However, if the human body ingests excess preservatives, human health will be adversely affected. Therefore, the detection of preservatives in food is important [134]. Dong et al. prepared a fluorescent film to detect chloramphenicol in chickens [135]. A calcium carbonate fluorescent nanocomposite film was synthesized by coprecipitating CDs on the surface of carboxymethyl cellulose. The composite has good fluorescence properties, colloid stability, and dispersion. The minimum detection limit of chloramphenicol in chickens was shown to be 0.03 μg/kg. A fluorescent probe was prepared using similar method, which has the advantages of easy synthesis, simple modification, low preparation cost, and rapid, simple, and sensitive detection. Melamine is an organic compound that is harmful to the body and is prohibited from being added to food. Room temperature phosphorescent (RTP) probe makes the detection visualization clear and convenient. Kong et al. embedded nitride-doped CDs (CNQDs) in a sodium carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC) matrix to form CNQDs-CMC thin films [136]. The composite films of CNQDs-CMC display RTP property and have been used to detect melamine (MEL); the fluorescence spectra and linearity of CNQDS-CMC films treated with different concentrations of MEL are shown in Fig. 13 (a–c). The yellow RTP color of the CNQDS-CMC film is quenched in the presence of MEL, and the quenching can be photographed by smartphone. The change can be identified by the color recognition app on a smartphone, as shown in Fig. 13 (d), which achieves the quantitative detection of MEL. Moreover, the composite film has high selectivity for MEL (Fig. 13 (e)). Therefore, using the visible RTP color change of the CNQDS-CMC film, the digital image colorimetry determination of MEL was realized. In summary, visual detection of organic matter may increase the safety of people's living environment. However, due to the diverse chemical nature of the samples at room temperature, such as solids, liquids, and gases, the detection of organic matter using cellulose-based fluorescent CDs composites is usually conducted in aqueous solution; thus, a great challenge is in the visual detection of most organic matter. We predict that the range of applications for cellulose fluorescent CD composites might be expanded with the development of fluorescent aerogel composites for the detection of organic gases, and the detection of gaseous organic matter will become a research hotspot in the future.

In the information age, data security is particularly important in the economic and high-security fields, as well as in our daily lives [137,138]. The demand for anti-counterfeiting technologies is increasing to ensure the security and integrity of paper-based documents. Recently, a fluorescent ink with good pH-responsive optical properties has been developed, which shows a wide range of application prospects [112]. When the ink was exposed to the atmosphere of hydrochloric acid gas, the fluorescent color of the pattern obtained by anti-counterfeiting ink writing quickly changed to cyan. When the above pattern was sequentially exposed to ammonia atmosphere, the color of the fluorescent pattern quickly reverted to blue. After 30 repetitions, the fluorescent color of these patterns could still be quickly and reversibly converted, as shown in Fig. 14 (a). Furtherly, a dual fluorescent ink was created, which have a wider range of fluorescence. Li et al. doped rare earth elements Yb and Er into CQDs and successfully grafted them onto dialdehyde NFC (DANFC) through a reductive amine reaction, thus designing a water-based fluorescent dual anti-counterfeit ink (Yb/Er-CQDs-DANFC), as shown in Fig. 14 (b) [139]. After the addition of CNF, the thixotropy and yield stress of the aqueous ink increase significantly, and the composite emerges fluorescence at both 550 nm and 980 nm, which achieves a dual anti-counterfeiting effect. Different shapes of graphic patterns wrote by the Yb/Er-CQDs-DANFC were printed on the paper, including two-dimensional code patterns and art pictures, which is invisible in daylight, but shows blue fluorescence under UV light, as shown in Fig. 14 (c)–(f).

On the other hand, the potential information on the surface of different materials may be extracted by measuring fluorescence, and the current cellulose-based CDs fluorescence composites are promising in this field [140–142]. Latent fingerprints (LFPs) may exist in a wide range of substances, which creates some difficulties to extract and detect them. The flexibility of a fluorescence composite enables the transfer of potential fingerprints and provides great convenience for the security field. A fluorescent nanofibrillated cellulose/carbon dots (NFC/CDs) composite paper was created by growing fluorescent CDs on the surface of NFC with microwave treatment. Meanwhile, the NFC/CD paper may be used to solve the problem of simultaneous and efficient transfer and detection of LFPs on different substrates, as shown in Fig. 14 (e) [45]. The efficiency of LFPs transfer and detection was evaluated by feature point extraction and similarity analysis. Fluorescent nanopaper can transfer most of the feature points from the substrate surface to the NFC/CD paper with a similarity of more than 60%, and retains the details of fingerprint patterns, such as ridges, sweat pores and feature points, thus achieving high-efficiency latent fingerprint detection (Fig. 14 (h)). Moreover, the NFC/CD paper has the advantages of high mechanical strength, flexibility, excellent optical properties, and good wettability. In conclusion, the renewable nature and biocompatibility of cellulose and CDs allow the composite to be used not only for fluorescent inks but also for other applications, such as food packaging [112], banknote security printing [143], and information encryption [142].

Both CDs and cellulose have good biocompatibility, and CD/cellulose fluorescent composites are very promising and potential biomaterials for bioimaging, biodrug delivery, and tumor therapy [144–146]. A hybrid nanomaterial was prepared to investigate the potential applications in cell imaging [147]. In this report, amino-functionalized CQDs (NH2-CQDs) were synthesized using a microwave method, and TEMPO-oxidized cellulose nanocrystals (TO-CNCs) were prepared using hydrochloric acid hydrolysis and TEMPO-mediated oxidation. Then, the NH2-CQDs and TO-CNC were coupled by carbodiimide-assisted coupling chemistry to prepare TO-CNC@CQD hybrid material. By investigating the interaction of TO-CNC@CQD with RAW 264.7 macrophages in vitro, cell viability tests show that the surface coupling of TO-CNC with NH2-CQDs not only improves the cytocompatibility of TO-CNC but also enhance the cytosolic association and internalization of TO-CNC in RAW 264.7 cells. Fig. 15 (a)-(b) present the confocal fluorescence micrographs of RAW 264.7 macrophages incubated with NH2-CQDs, AF488-CNCs, and TO-CNC@ CQD hybrids at 37 °C for 4 h and 24 h, respectively. Based on these results, the TO-CNC@CQD hybrid nanomaterial has far-reaching potential for bioimaging applications. Sarkar et al. synthesized CD-conjugated carboxymethylcellulose (CMC)-hydroxyapatite (HA) nanocomposites (CDs-CMC-HA) by a one-pot method, and then loaded the nanocomposites with the chemotherapeutic drug adriamycin (DOX) to evaluate the drug loading potential of the synthesized nanocomposites [124], as shown in Fig. 15 (c). The anticancer drug DOX had been successfully loaded onto CDs-CMC-HA nanocomposites with a high loading capacity (36.6%) and loading efficiency (88.35%). The CDs-CMC-HA composites exhibit a good ability to induce the differentiation of MG63 cells and high alkaline phosphatase activity.

Local delivery of chemotherapeutic drugs using an implantable drug delivery system is a promising strategy for the treatment of malignant brain tumors. Nucleus/chitosan-polyethylene oxide-carbon quantum dots/carboxymethyl cellulose-polyvinyl alcohol (CS-PEO-CQDs/CMC-PVA) nanofibers were successfully prepared using coaxial electrostatic spinning and used as degradable polymer implants for local delivery of temozolomide (TMZ) [41]. After the initial drug release, sustained drug release of TMZ was observed for up to 28 days at different pH values and drug loadings, as shown in Fig. 16 (a)-(d). Ulvan (UN) is a drug with antioxidant, anti-inflammatory and anticancer properties and usually used to treat chronic gastroenteritis. Chen et al. prepared CDs/hematite α-Fe2O3 quantum dots (HQDs)/hydroxypropyl cellulose cross-linked chitosan (HPCCS)/UN nanoparticles (UN NPs), denoted CD-HQDs/HPCCS/UN NPs, and investigated the potential of the composite particles for applications in drug delivery of chronic gastroenteritis [148]. In the experiment, the cumulative release of UN was measured at pH 2.0, 6.0 and 7.4 by simulating the environment of gastric fluid, infected sites, and intestinal fluid, respectively. The cumulative release rates of UN from CD-HQDs/HPCCS/UN NPs are 23.45%, 30.51% and 73.4% at pH 2.0, 6.0 and 7.4, respectively, as shown in Fig. 16 (e). Compared with pH 2.0 and 6.0, the 20-h release rate (83.32%) of NPs is higher at pH 7.4 (Fig. 16 (f)). The cytotoxic effects of CD-HQDs/HPCCS/UN NPs on A549 cells also show that the NPs have significant cytotoxicity in the cells, and the property is good for killing cancer cells (Fig. 16 (g)). Therefore, the composite materials formed from cellulose and CDs have a wide range of prospective application in the field of biology. However, most of the relevant studies are still in the early stages of development, and relatively few biological experiments have been conducted in clinical medicine.

With recent research, cellulose-based fluorescent carbon dot composites have attracted extensive attention, but some challenges persist, and more efforts are needed. First, high-performance CDs must be developed. The yield and quantum yield of CDs prepared are currently lower than those of other traditional fluorescent substances, which hinders the development of CDs, particularly using biomass as a precursor [47,57,60,61,150]. Meanwhile, the fluorescence emission of obtained CDs is currently mostly blue and green, and other colors of CDs are rarely obtained, which limits their further development. In addition, other high-quality CDs, such as high fluorescent solid-state CDs, thermal activation delayed fluorescent CDs, and room temperature phosphorescent CDs, still need further development to meet the requirements of high-performance cellulose-based fluorescence composites. Second, the production and properties of cellulose and its derivatives with different dimensional forms must be improved because of their high energy consumption, decreasing hydrophobicity, and poor stability. Moreover, cellulose hydrogel is less recyclable in the application process. Finally, based on the aforementioned analyses, the strength and number of bonds between cellulose and CDs is key to improving the performance of the cellulose-based fluorescent composite. However, CDs are easily removed when they are combined with cellulose or its derivatives for two main reasons. On the one hand, the functional groups on the CDs surface are diverse, and cellulose and its derivatives only can interact with a few functional groups on the CDs surface. Moreover, an appropriate number of CDs combined with cellulose might weaken the aggregation caused by the quenching effect and enhance the fluorescence properties of CDs, but excessive CDs combined with the cellulose skeleton are not conducive to the fluorescence emission of CDs. If quantitative binding between CDs and cellulose is achieved, it will be a major breakthrough for the preparation of high-performance cellulose-based fluorescence composite materials. On the other hand, CDs are not stable when they are combined with cellulose and its derivatives through electrostatic interactions or hydrogen bond interactions, leading to CDs shedding easily due to the influence of external environment. Therefore, the binding strength between CDs and cellulose must be strengthened.
